Description: Low purchasing power of citizens and high share of low-income pensioners; Weak financial capacity of local self-government units; Lack of developed and tested financing models for renovation investments other than grants; Long return on investment due to low energy prices and extensive use of firewood and high construction costs; Necessity of structural renovation of buildings (and buildings damaged by earthquakes, for putting them into service and intact buildings, to ensure the safety of the use of the building) which cannot be covered by energy savings

Shortage of skilled labour – a key barrier that partly generates both financial recommendations (high labour costs) and contributes to slower renovation dynamics as well as potential quality issues of the works carried out

Complex procedures for using land owned by local self-government units and obtaining the necessary consent (in built-up urban areas, often the physical space in the immediate vicinity of the building, needed to house the system, is not private property and requires a complex procedure for obtaining prior consent for exploration works (where needed) and consent for land servitude); Lengthy approval procedures for grid connection (increase of connection power for heat pumps, connection of photovoltaic power plants to the distribution network); Lengthy public procurement processes in the public sector, often with unsatisfactory results; Unresolved property-law relationships for buildings; Complexity of the multidisciplinary approach to renovation

Category: Barriers related to the level of awareness
Description: Lack of public awareness and education on the wider benefits of energy renovation; Failure to comply with procedures relating to renewal procedures and the use of related grants (necessary technical assistance); Citizens' low motivation to renovate without a grant (linked to financial barriers, i.e. an inability to cover renovation costs by themselves); Habits or necessity of partial space heating and extensive use of firewood (related to citizens' poor financial condition)

Complexity of decision-making in multi-apartment buildings; Demographic and migration trends – emigration and increasing number of buildings left unoccupied; Regional property price differences – renovation is equally expensive, but the value of the renovated property varies significantly by location; Uneven market development and diverging interests of stakeholders in the renovation process
